result:
I keep coming back to the idea that everything is reheating.  
Not just the cosmic microwave background—though that too is warming by a few microkelvin every eon as the universe keeps expanding and the photons keep red-shifting into longer, lazier wavelengths.  
I mean the small stuff: the way yesterday’s coffee in the mug on my desk is reheated by proximity to my palm, the way old arguments get warmed over at dinner tables, the way grief is microwaved in the brain late at night until it bubbles up the edges and you have to open the door of your skull fast so it doesn’t spill over.

There’s a second law pun somewhere in here about entropy and the kitchen, but I’m trying to resist it.  
Instead I picture the first time I saw frost form on the inside of a windowpane: I was seven, tracing a spaceship with my fingernail, and the white film sublimated off the glass exactly the way memories sublimate off childhood—first a ghost outline, then nothing but a water stain you can’t be sure was ever deliberate.  
That same window is still in my parents’ house, but the tree on the other side of it has grown so high it scratches the gutter like an old vinyl record. I imagine the house listening to that branch, needle in groove, playing the same skip every windy night for twenty-five years.

I read once that trees make the wind.  
Not metaphorically—literally. Their leaves transpire moisture, the vapor rises, pressure gradients form, air moves.  
So when you stand under a big maple and feel a breeze, you’re feeling the tree’s exhalation, a lung bigger than your body, older than your first heartbeat.  
I keep that fact folded in my wallet like one of those tiny cheat sheets students smuggle into exams.  
Whenever the day feels multiple-choice and all answers seem equally wrong, I unfold it, reread it, remember that respiration goes both ways: trees exhale wind, we exhale carbon, the planet keeps grading us on a curve we can’t see.

Tonight the sky is a chalkboard someone forgot to erase.  
Orion is half drawn, the Pleiades smudged thumbprints, and the moon is that kid in the front row who raises his hand even when he doesn’t know the answer—pale, eager, unavoidably obvious.  
I wave back.  
Who am I to leave a celestial body hanging?

I keep thinking I’ll run out of words, but they regenerate the way mealworms supposedly can digest polystyrene: improbably, hungrily, turning trash into motion.  
That’s what tonight feels like—chewing through the plastic packaging of the day, converting it into legs.  
Somewhere a dumpster of unread notifications is rusting, and inside it my unread condolences, my unliked compliments, my unopened receipt for the sweater I bought the week before the funeral.  
All of it breaking down into smaller beads of neon that will still be glowing long after the sun balloons into a red giant and Mercury becomes a toasted marshmallow.

But here’s the secret: the reheating is optional.  
You can let the coffee go cold; the argument can stay freezer-burned; the grief can remain uncooked, a shrink-wrapped drumstick you never have to eat if you don’t want to.  
The second law isn’t a commandment, just a weather report.  
And weather, as any tree will tell you, is only the temporary shape of wanting.
